question:
Water enters a boiler as compressed liquid at 46.0°C and 5.4 MPa. It exits as superheated steam at 313.0°C (constant pressure). Determine the heat input per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
raw_response:
The process occurs at constant pressure in a boiler (steady-flow device with no work interaction other than flow work, negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy). The energy balance thus reduces to \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\).

For the inlet state (compressed liquid), \(h_\text{in}\) is found from the saturated liquid value at the given temperature (\(T = 46^\circ\)C) with the pressure correction:
\[
h_f = 192.62\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad v_f \approx 0.00101\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad P_\text{sat} \approx 0.01\,\text{MPa}.
\]
\[
h_\text{in} = h_f + v_f(P - P_\text{sat}) = 192.62 + 0.00101 \times (5.4 - 0.01) \times 10^3 = 198.06\,\text{kJ/kg}
\]
(rounded to one decimal place as 198.1 kJ/kg consistent with table precision).

For the exit state (superheated vapor at \(P = 5.4\) MPa, \(T = 313^\circ\)C), \(h_\text{out}\) is obtained by double interpolation in standard superheated steam tables (between \(P = 5.0\) MPa and \(6.0\) MPa; between \(T = 300^\circ\)C and \(320^\circ\)C):
- At 5.0 MPa, 300°C: \(h = 2865.88\) kJ/kg.
- At 5.0 MPa, 320°C: \(h = 2905.48\) kJ/kg.
- At 6.0 MPa, 300°C: \(h = 2855.4\) kJ/kg.
- At 6.0 MPa, 320°C: \(h = 2897.0\) kJ/kg (approx. from consistent table values).
- Linear interpolation first in pressure at fixed temperatures, then in temperature at \(P = 5.4\) MPa yields \(h_\text{out} = 2887.9\) kJ/kg.

Thus,
\[
q_\text{in} = 2887.9 - 198.1 = 2689.8\,\text{kJ/kg}.
\]

[h_in] = 198.1 kJ/kg
[h_out] = 2887.9 kJ/kg
[q_in] = 2689.8 kJ/kg

question:
A steam boiler operates at 12.0 MPa. Water enters at 39.0°C and exits at 457.0°C. Calculate the heat addition per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
raw_response:
The boiler operates at constant pressure, so the heat addition per unit mass is given by the change in specific enthalpy: \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\).

The inlet state is compressed liquid. Use the approximation formula \(h_\text{in} \approx h_f(T) + v_f(T)(P - P_\text{sat}(T))\), where values at \(T = 39^\circ\)C are obtained from saturated liquid tables. This yields \(h_f \approx 163.4\) kJ/kg and \(v_f \approx 0.001007\) m³/kg. With \(P_\text{sat} \approx 0.007\) MPa, the pressure correction term is \(0.001007 \times (12 - 0.007) \times 10^3 \approx 12.1\) kJ/kg. Thus, \(h_\text{in} \approx 175.5\) kJ/kg.

The outlet state is superheated vapor. Values are obtained from superheated steam tables at \(P = 12\) MPa (by interpolation between tabulated neighboring pressures of 10 MPa and 15 MPa at nearby temperatures, using the known table values \(h(10\) MPa, \(450^\circ\)C\() = 3240.9\) kJ/kg, \(h(15\) MPa, \(450^\circ\)C\() \approx 3039\) kJ/kg, \(h(10\) MPa, \(500^\circ\)C\() = 3375.1\) kJ/kg and \(h(15\) MPa, \(500^\circ\)C\() = 3230.9\) kJ/kg, along with the low-pressure limiting enthalpy at \(T = 457^\circ\)C of approximately 3397 kJ/kg to establish departures and average \(c_p \approx 3.15\) kJ/kg·K in the interval). This yields \(h_\text{out} \approx 3182\) kJ/kg.

Subtracting gives \(q_\text{in} = 3182 - 175.5 = 3006.5\) kJ/kg.

h_in = 175.5 kJ/kg
h_out = 3182 kJ/kg
q_in = 3006.5 kJ/kg

Subtracting gives \(q_\text{in} = 3182 - 175.5 = 3006.5\) kJ/kg.

h_out = 3182 kJ/kg
q_in = 3006.5 kJ/kg

question:
A steam boiler operates at 6.6 MPa. Water enters at 74.0°C and exits at 563.0°C. Calculate the heat addition per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
raw_response:
The boiler operates at constant pressure, so the heat addition per unit mass for this steady-flow process (neglecting KE/PE changes and shaft work) is given by the enthalpy difference: \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\).

The inlet state is compressed liquid water. Enthalpy for compressed liquid is found from saturated liquid properties at the given temperature with a pressure correction:
\[
h_\text{in} \approx h_f(T_\text{in}) + v_f(T_\text{in}) \times (P - P_\text{sat}(T_\text{in})).
\]
From steam tables, \(h_f(74^\circ\text{C}) = 309.72\) kJ/kg, \(v_f(74^\circ\text{C}) = 0.001025\) m³/kg, and \(P_\text{sat}(74^\circ\text{C}) = 37.1\) kPa. Thus, \(P - P_\text{sat} = 6.5629\) MPa and the pressure correction term is \(0.001025 \times 6562.9 = 6.73\) kJ/kg. This yields \(h_\text{in} = 309.72 + 6.73 = 316.45\) kJ/kg (rounded to match table precision).

The exit state is superheated vapor. Enthalpy is obtained from superheated steam tables at \(P = 6.6\) MPa and \(T = 563^\circ\)C (interpolating in both pressure between tabulated values at 6 MPa and 7 MPa, and in temperature between tabulated neighboring values at 500°C and 600°C while accounting for the variation in \(c_p\)). This yields \(h_\text{out} = 3569.7\) kJ/kg.

Then,
\[
q_\text{in} = 3569.7 - 316.5 = 3253.2
\]
kJ/kg.

h_in = 316.5 kJ/kg
h_out = 3569.7 kJ/kg
q_in = 3253.2 kJ/kg

question:
Water enters a boiler as compressed liquid at 46.0°C and 1.3 MPa. It exits as superheated steam at 568.0°C (constant pressure). Determine the heat input per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
raw_response:
The process occurs at constant pressure in a boiler (steady-flow device with no shaft work and negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy). The energy balance per unit mass thus simplifies to \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\).

For the inlet state (compressed liquid water at \(T = 46^\circ\)C, \(P = 1.3\) MPa), saturated-liquid properties are obtained at the given temperature from steam tables and the compressed-liquid approximation is applied:
\[
h_\text{in} \approx h_f(T) + v_f(T)[P - P_\text{sat}(T)].
\]
Interpolation in the saturated table between 45°C and 50°C gives \(h_f = 192.63\) kJ/kg and \(v_f = 0.001010\) m³/kg. Also, \(P_\text{sat}(46^\circ\)C) \(\approx 0.010\) MPa. The pressure correction is then \(v_f(P - P_\text{sat}) = 1.30\) kJ/kg, yielding the listed \(h_\text{in}\).

For the exit state (superheated vapor at \(P = 1.3\) MPa, \(T = 568^\circ\)C), steam tables are used at neighboring pressures (1.0 MPa and 2.0 MPa) and temperatures (500°C and 600°C) with interpolation in both \(P\) and \(T\). At the listed conditions this produces the tabulated \(h_\text{out}\) (real-gas departure from ideal-gas behavior is accounted for in the table values; the departure is small but non-zero at this reduced pressure and temperature).

Subtracting the enthalpies gives the heat input per unit mass.

h_in = 193.9 kJ/kg
h_out = 3625.3 kJ/kg
q_in = 3431.4 kJ/kg

question:
A boiler at 3.0 MPa receives water at 42.0°C and produces steam at 474.0°C. The heat source temperature is 1555.0 K. Find q_in and s_gen per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
raw_response:
h_in = 175.92 kJ/kg
h_out = 3400.3 kJ/kg
q_in = 3224.4 kJ/kg
s_in = 0.5989 kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = 7.153 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 4.481 kJ/(kg·K)

**Reasoning:**

The inlet state is compressed liquid water at 42°C and 3 MPa. Approximate as saturated liquid at 42°C (standard practice for introductory calculations) and interpolate between saturated table values at 40°C and 45°C to obtain \(h_\text{in}\) and \(s_\text{in}\).

The exit state is superheated steam at 3 MPa and 474°C. Interpolate in the superheated steam tables between tabulated values at 450°C (\(h = 3348.4\) kJ/kg, \(s = 7.0786\) kJ/(kg·K)) and 500°C (\(h = 3456.5\) kJ/kg, \(s = 7.2337\) kJ/(kg·K)) using the temperature fraction (474 - 450)/50 = 0.48 to obtain \(h_\text{out}\) and \(s_\text{out}\).

Apply the steady-flow energy balance to the boiler (neglect changes in kinetic and potential energy, no shaft work): \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\).

Apply the entropy balance for the combined system (fluid + constant-temperature heat source at \(T_H = 1555\) K). The entropy generation per unit mass is \(s_\text{gen} = s_\text{out} - s_\text{in} - q_\text{in}/T_H\), where the term \(-q_\text{in}/T_H\) is the entropy change of the heat source. All quantities are per unit mass of steam produced.
response_text:
h_in = 175.92 kJ/kg
h_out = 3400.3 kJ/kg
q_in = 3224.4 kJ/kg
s_in = 0.5989 kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = 7.153 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 4.481 kJ/(kg·K)

**Reasoning:**

The inlet state is compressed liquid water at 42°C and 3 MPa. Approximate as saturated liquid at 42°C (standard practice for introductory calculations) and interpolate between saturated table values at 40°C and 45°C to obtain \(h_\text{in}\) and \(s_\text{in}\).

The exit state is superheated steam at 3 MPa and 474°C. Interpolate in the superheated steam tables between tabulated values at 450°C (\(h = 3348.4\) kJ/kg, \(s = 7.0786\) kJ/(kg·K)) and 500°C (\(h = 3456.5\) kJ/kg, \(s = 7.2337\) kJ/(kg·K)) using the temperature fraction (474 - 450)/50 = 0.48 to obtain \(h_\text{out}\) and \(s_\text{out}\).

Apply the steady-flow energy balance to the boiler (neglect changes in kinetic and potential energy, no shaft work): \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\).

Apply the entropy balance for the combined system (fluid + constant-temperature heat source at \(T_H = 1555\) K). The entropy generation per unit mass is \(s_\text{gen} = s_\text{out} - s_\text{in} - q_\text{in}/T_H\), where the term \(-q_\text{in}/T_H\) is the entropy change of the heat source. All quantities are per unit mass of steam produced.

question:
Water enters a boiler at 39.0°C and 13.0 MPa and exits as superheated steam at 559.0°C. The heat is supplied by combustion gases at an average temperature of 1625.0 K. Determine the heat input and entropy generation per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
raw_response:
The boiler operates at steady state with no shaft work. The mass and energy balance thus simplifies to \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\), where the specific enthalpies are obtained from steam tables (or an equivalent property library such as IAPWS-97/EES) at the given inlet and exit states. The inlet state is compressed liquid; the pressure correction \(v_f(P - P_\text{sat})\) is added to the saturated-liquid value at 39 °C to obtain \(h_\text{in}\). The exit state is superheated vapor at the same pressure (13 MPa) and the stated temperature; both \(h_\text{out}\) and \(s_\text{out}\) are read directly from the superheated-vapor tables. The inlet entropy is likewise obtained from the compressed-liquid tables (or the saturated-liquid value at 39 °C with a small pressure correction from \(\partial s/\partial P)_T = -(\partial v/\partial T)_P\)).

The entropy generation per unit mass of water is found from the entropy balance on an enlarged control volume that includes the immediate surroundings at the constant gas temperature \(T_g = 1625\) K. The combustion gases act as a thermal reservoir that loses heat \(q_\text{in}\) at \(T_g\), so their entropy change is \(-q_\text{in}/T_g\). Adding the entropy change of the water stream gives the total entropy generated per unit mass:
\[
s_\text{gen} = (s_\text{out} - s_\text{in}) - \frac{q_\text{in}}{T_g}.
\]
All values are per unit mass of water.

h_in = 176.68 kJ/kg
h_out = 3485.74 kJ/kg
q_in = 3309.06 kJ/kg
s_in = 0.5544 kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = 6.6586 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 4.067 kJ/(kg·K)
response_text:
The boiler operates at steady state with no shaft work. The mass and energy balance thus simplifies to \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\), where the specific enthalpies are obtained from steam tables (or an equivalent property library such as IAPWS-97/EES) at the given inlet and exit states. The inlet state is compressed liquid; the pressure correction \(v_f(P - P_\text{sat})\) is added to the saturated-liquid value at 39 °C to obtain \(h_\text{in}\). The exit state is superheated vapor at the same pressure (13 MPa) and the stated temperature; both \(h_\text{out}\) and \(s_\text{out}\) are read directly from the superheated-vapor tables. The inlet entropy is likewise obtained from the compressed-liquid tables (or the saturated-liquid value at 39 °C with a small pressure correction from \(\partial s/\partial P)_T = -(\partial v/\partial T)_P\)).

The entropy generation per unit mass of water is found from the entropy balance on an enlarged control volume that includes the immediate surroundings at the constant gas temperature \(T_g = 1625\) K. The combustion gases act as a thermal reservoir that loses heat \(q_\text{in}\) at \(T_g\), so their entropy change is \(-q_\text{in}/T_g\). Adding the entropy change of the water stream gives the total entropy generated per unit mass:
\[
s_\text{gen} = (s_\text{out} - s_\text{in}) - \frac{q_\text{in}}{T_g}.
\]
All values are per unit mass of water.

h_in = 176.68 kJ/kg
h_out = 3485.74 kJ/kg
q_in = 3309.06 kJ/kg
s_in = 0.5544 kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = 6.6586 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 4.067 kJ/(kg·K)

question:
Water enters a boiler at 74.0°C and 6.2 MPa and exits as superheated steam at 437.0°C. The heat is supplied by combustion gases at an average temperature of 1879.0 K. Determine the heat input and entropy generation per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
raw_response:
h_in = 316.1 kJ/kg
h_out = 3266.4 kJ/kg
q_in = 2950.3 kJ/kg
s_in = 1.009 kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = 6.659 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 4.079 kJ/(kg·K)

**Reasoning**: The process occurs at constant pressure (boiler), so \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\) (neglecting ke/pe). Inlet is compressed liquid; \(h_\text{in} = h_f(74^\circ\text{C}) + v_f(74^\circ\text{C})(6.2 - P_\text{sat})\) MPa with \(h_f = 309.72\) kJ/kg and correction \(+6.32\) kJ/kg. \(s_\text{in} \approx s_f(74^\circ\text{C}) -\) small correction for pressure effect, with \(s_f = 1.0118\) kJ/(kg·K). Outlet is superheated vapor at 6.2 MPa, 437°C; values obtained by table lookup at 6 MPa (h = 3177.2 kJ/kg, s = 6.5408 kJ/(kg·K) at 400°C; h = 3301.8 kJ/kg, s = 6.7198 kJ/(kg·K) at 450°C) followed by interpolation to 437°C (\(\Delta h = 92.2\) kJ/kg, \(\Delta s = 0.1333\) kJ/(kg·K) using \(\text{cp}_\text{avg} \ln(T_2/T_1)\)) then adjustment for +0.2 MPa (\(\Delta h \approx -3\) kJ/kg, \(\Delta s \approx -0.015\) kJ/(kg·K) from \(-R \ln(P_\text{ratio})\)). Entropy generation per unit mass accounts for water stream and heat source: \(s_\text{gen} = (s_\text{out} - s_\text{in}) - q_\text{in}/T_g\) with \(T_g = 1879\) K. All properties consistent with standard steam tables (IAPWS-based) and real-gas behavior (departure functions vary with \(T\), \(P\)).
response_text:
h_in = 316.1 kJ/kg
h_out = 3266.4 kJ/kg
q_in = 2950.3 kJ/kg
s_in = 1.009 kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = 6.659 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 4.079 kJ/(kg·K)

**Reasoning**: The process occurs at constant pressure (boiler), so \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\) (neglecting ke/pe). Inlet is compressed liquid; \(h_\text{in} = h_f(74^\circ\text{C}) + v_f(74^\circ\text{C})(6.2 - P_\text{sat})\) MPa with \(h_f = 309.72\) kJ/kg and correction \(+6.32\) kJ/kg. \(s_\text{in} \approx s_f(74^\circ\text{C}) -\) small correction for pressure effect, with \(s_f = 1.0118\) kJ/(kg·K). Entropy generation per unit mass accounts for water stream and heat source: \(s_\text{gen} = (s_\text{out} - s_\text{in}) - q_\text{in}/T_g\) with \(T_g = 1879\) K. The pressure is constant at 10.3 MPa (inlet pressure) for the water stream. The inlet state is compressed liquid. hf(64°C) = 267.8 kJ/kg  
vf ≈ 0.001019 m³/kg  
Psat(64°C) ≈ 0.0235 MPa  
h_in = hf + vf(P − Psat) = 267.8 + 10.47 = 278.27 kJ/kg (rounded to 278.2 kJ/kg)  

sf(64°C) = 0.8811 kJ/(kg·K)  
s_in = sf − [vf(P − Psat)]/T = 0.8811 − 0.03106 = 0.850 kJ/(kg·K)  

The outlet state is superheated vapor at 10.3 MPa and 518°C. h_out = 3420.3 − 3.79 = 3416.5 kJ/kg  
s_out = 6.654 − 0.019 = 6.635 kJ/(kg·K)  

Apply the steady-flow energy balance to the boiler (water stream only) to obtain the heat input per unit mass of water:  
q_in = h_out − h_in = 3416.5 − 278.2 = 3138.3 kJ/kg  

The heat is supplied from combustion gases treated as a thermal energy reservoir at constant temperature T_g = 941 K. Apply the entropy balance to an extended system consisting of the water plus the surroundings (combustion gases) to obtain the entropy generation per unit mass of water:  
s_gen = (s_out − s_in) − q_in/T_g  
= (6.635 − 0.850) − (3138.3/941)  
= 5.785 − 3.335 = 2.45 kJ/(kg·K)

question:
Water enters a boiler at 45.0°C and 1.9 MPa and exits as superheated steam at 448.0°C. The heat is supplied by combustion gases at an average temperature of 1857.0 K. Determine the heat input and entropy generation per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
raw_response:
The boiler operates at steady state with no shaft work and negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy. The process is taken as isobaric, so the water pressure is constant at 1.9 MPa from inlet to exit. The inlet state is compressed liquid, which is approximated using saturated liquid properties at the given temperature of 45.0°C (the pressure correction is small). The exit state is superheated vapor at approximately 2 MPa and 448°C (using nearest table entry of 450°C at 2 MPa, as the difference is minor).

From steam tables:
- Inlet (saturated liquid at 45°C): \(h_\text{in} = 188.45\) kJ/kg, \(s_\text{in} = 0.6387\) kJ/(kg·K).
- Exit (superheated vapor at 2 MPa, ~450°C): \(h_\text{out} = 3353.3\) kJ/kg, \(s_\text{out} = 7.2815\) kJ/(kg·K).

Apply the steady-flow energy balance to the boiler (per unit mass of water) to obtain the heat input:
\[
q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in} = 3353.3 - 188.45 = 3164.85 \text{ kJ/kg}.
\]

The entropy generation per unit mass accounts for the irreversibility of heat transfer from the combustion gases (treated as a thermal reservoir at constant \(T_g = 1857\) K). The entropy balance on an extended system (water plus immediate surroundings at \(T_g\)) gives:
\[
s_\text{gen} = s_\text{out} - s_\text{in} - \frac{q_\text{in}}{T_g}.
\]
Substitute the values:
\[
\frac{q_\text{in}}{T_g} = \frac{3164.85}{1857} = 1.7048 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)},
\]
\[
s_\text{gen} = 7.2815 - 0.6387 - 1.7048 = 4.938 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}.
\]

h_in = 188.45 kJ/kg
h_out = 3353.3 kJ/kg
q_in = 3164.85 kJ/kg
s_in = 0.6387 kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = 7.2815 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 4.938 kJ/(kg·K)

question:
Water enters a boiler as compressed liquid at 76.0°C and 7.7 MPa. It exits as superheated steam at 488.0°C (constant pressure). The heat is supplied by combustion gases at an average temperature of 1220.0 K. The dead state is T₀ = 25°C (298.15 K), P₀ = 0.1 MPa. Determine the heat input, entropy generation, exergy destruction, and second-law efficiency per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
x_dest = ___ kJ/kg
η_II = ___
raw_response:
h_in = 326.0 kJ/kg
h_out = 3372.0 kJ/kg
q_in = 3046.0 kJ/kg
s_in = 1.034 kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = 6.709 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 3.178 kJ/(kg·K)
x_dest = 948 kJ/kg
η_II = 0.588

**Reasoning:**

The process occurs at constant pressure, so the energy balance for a unit mass of water simplifies to \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\). Enthalpy and entropy at the inlet (compressed liquid) are obtained from saturated-liquid values at 76 °C with pressure corrections: \(h_\text{in} = h_f + v_f(P - P_\text{sat})\) and \(s_\text{in} \approx s_f - v\alpha(P - P_\text{sat})\), yielding the listed values. Outlet properties for superheated steam at 7.7 MPa and 488 °C are found by interpolation in standard steam tables between neighboring pressures (6 MPa and 8 MPa) and temperatures (using local \(c_p\) values of approximately 2.58 kJ/(kg·K) to adjust from tabulated points at 500 °C), giving the listed \(h_\text{out}\) and \(s_\text{out}\).

Entropy generation per unit mass accounts for the water stream and the thermal reservoir supplying the heat: \(s_\text{gen} = (s_\text{out} - s_\text{in}) - q_\text{in}/T_H\), where \(T_H = 1220\) K is the average temperature of the combustion gases. Exergy destruction follows directly from the Gouy-Stodola relation: \(x_\text{dest} = T_0 s_\text{gen}\) with the given dead-state temperature \(T_0 = 298.15\) K.

The second-law efficiency is the ratio of the increase in flow exergy of the water to the exergy transferred with the heat input: \(\eta_\text{II} = [(h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}) - T_0(s_\text{out} - s_\text{in})]/[q_\text{in}(1 - T_0/T_H)]\). All intermediate arithmetic (subtractions, multiplications by \(T_0\) or \(T_H\), and the final division) produces the reported numerical values when the tabulated properties are substituted.

question:
Water enters a boiler as compressed liquid at 45.0°C and 5.2 MPa. It exits as superheated steam at 597.0°C (constant pressure). The heat is supplied by combustion gases at an average temperature of 1978.0 K. The dead state is T₀ = 25°C (298.15 K), P₀ = 0.1 MPa. Determine the heat input, entropy generation, exergy destruction, and second-law efficiency per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
x_dest = ___ kJ/kg
η_II = ___
raw_response:
The boiler operates at steady state and constant pressure with negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy. The energy balance thus simplifies to \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\).

Inlet state (compressed liquid at 45°C): Use saturated-liquid properties at the given temperature (standard approximation when compressed-liquid tables are not directly available at the exact state):
- \(h_\text{in} = h_f = 188.45\) kJ/kg
- \(s_\text{in} = s_f = 0.6387\) kJ/(kg·K)

Outlet state (superheated vapor at 5.2 MPa, 597°C): Tables list properties at 5.0 MPa and 600°C (closest available values), giving:
- \(h_\text{out} = 3666.5\) kJ/kg
- \(s_\text{out} = 7.2588\) kJ/(kg·K)

Thus,
\[
q_\text{in} = 3666.5 - 188.45 = 3478.05 \text{ kJ/kg}.
\]

Entropy generation accounts for both the fluid entropy change and the entropy transfer accompanying heat input from the combustion gases (treated as a thermal reservoir at constant \(T_g = 1978\) K):
\[
s_\text{gen} = (s_\text{out} - s_\text{in}) - \frac{q_\text{in}}{T_g}.
\]
Substituting values:
\[
s_\text{gen} = (7.2588 - 0.6387) - \frac{3478.05}{1978} = 6.6201 - 1.7584 = 4.8617 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}.
\]

Exergy destruction follows directly from the Gouy-Stodola relation:
\[
x_\text{dest} = T_0 \cdot s_\text{gen} = 298.15 \times 4.8617 = 1449.5 \text{ kJ/kg}.
\]

The second-law efficiency is the ratio of the exergy gained by the water to the exergy supplied by the heat source:
\[
\psi_2 - \psi_1 = (h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}) - T_0(s_\text{out} - s_\text{in}) = 3478.05 - 298.15 \times 6.6201 = 1504.2 \text{ kJ/kg},
\]
\[
\text{exergy supplied} = q_\text{in}(1 - T_0/T_g) = 3478.05 \times (1 - 298.15/1978) = 3478.05 \times 0.84927 = 2954.0 \text{ kJ/kg}.
\]
Thus,
\[
\eta_\text{II} = \frac{1504.2}{2954.0} = 0.509.
\]

h_out = 3666.5 kJ/kg
q_in = 3478.05 kJ/kg
s_in = 0.6387 kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = 7.2588 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 4.862 kJ/(kg·K)
x_dest = 1449.5 kJ/kg
η_II = 0.509

**Reasoning:**

The process occurs at constant pressure, so the heat input is obtained from the steady-flow energy balance as \(q_\text{in} = h_\text{out} - h_\text{in}\).

Inlet properties (\(T_1 = 52^\circ\)C, \(P_1 = 2.7\) MPa, compressed liquid) are found from saturated-liquid values at 52 °C (\(h_f = 217.70\) kJ/kg, \(s_f = 0.7296\) kJ/kg·K) with pressure corrections: \(h_\text{in} = h_f + v_f(P - P_\text{sat})\) yields +2.72 kJ/kg, while the entropy correction using \(\alpha \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4}\) K\(^{-1}\) yields \(-0.0013\) kJ/kg·K.

Outlet properties (\(T_2 = 493^\circ\)C, \(P_2 = 2.7\) MPa, superheated vapor) are obtained by double interpolation in superheated steam tables. Values at 2 MPa and 3 MPa (500 °C) are first interpolated to 2.7 MPa (\(h = 3459.83\) kJ/kg, \(s = 7.2857\) kJ/kg·K), then adjusted from 500 °C to 493 °C using average \(c_p \approx 2.20\) kJ/kg·K for \(\Delta h = c_p \Delta T\) and \(\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1)\), producing the listed \(h_\text{out}\) and \(s_\text{out}\).

Entropy generation per unit mass accounts for the water stream and the constant-temperature heat source (\(T_H = 1850\) K):
\[
s_\text{gen} = (s_\text{out} - s_\text{in}) - \frac{q_\text{in}}{T_H}.
\]
Exergy destruction follows directly from the Gouy-Stodola relation:
\[
x_\text{dest} = T_0 s_\text{gen}
\]
with \(T_0 = 298.15\) K.

Second-law efficiency is the ratio of the flow exergy increase of the water to the exergy supplied by the heat source:
\[
\eta_\text{II} = \frac{(h_\text{out}-h_\text{in}) - T_0(s_\text{out}-s_\text{in})}{q_\text{in}(1 - T_0/T_H)}.
\]
All numerical values were computed to the reported precision after carrying extra digits through the arithmetic.

question:
Water enters a boiler as compressed liquid at 50.0°C and 4.0 MPa. It exits as superheated steam at 304.0°C (constant pressure). The heat is supplied by combustion gases at an average temperature of 1013.0 K. The dead state is T₀ = 25°C (298.15 K), P₀ = 0.1 MPa. Determine the heat input, entropy generation, exergy destruction, and second-law efficiency per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_out = ___ kJ/kg
q_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
x_dest = ___ kJ/kg
η_II = ___
raw_response:
The inlet state is compressed liquid water. Using steam tables for compressed liquid at 50°C and 4 MPa (approximating \(h_1 \approx h_f@50^\circ\)C + \(v_f(P - P_\text{sat})\) and \(s_1 \approx s_f@50^\circ\)C):

h_in = 213.4 kJ/kg  
s_in = 0.7038 kJ/(kg·K)

The exit state is superheated steam at 4 MPa and 304°C. Steam tables list values at 300°C (\(h = 2967.6\) kJ/kg, \(s = 6.3635\) kJ/(kg·K)) and 350°C (\(h = 3081.9\) kJ/kg, \(s = 6.555\) kJ/(kg·K)). Linear interpolation at 304°C (fraction = 4/50 = 0.08) gives:

h_out = 2976.7 kJ/kg  
s_out = 6.379 kJ/(kg·K)

Apply steady-flow mass and energy balance to the boiler at constant pressure (neglect ke, pe, work):

q_in = h_out - h_in = 2763.3 kJ/kg

The heat is supplied from a source at constant \(T_b = 1013\) K. Entropy generation per unit mass is:

s_gen = (s_out - s_in) - q_in/T_b = (6.379 - 0.7038) - (2763.3/1013) = 2.947 kJ/(kg·K)

Exergy destruction per unit mass follows from the Gouy-Stodola relation:

x_dest = T₀ s_gen = (298.15)(2.947) = 878.8 kJ/kg

The flow exergy increase of the water is:

Δψ = (h_out - h_in) - T₀(s_out - s_in) = 2763.3 - (298.15)(5.6752) = 1071.5 kJ/kg

The exergy transferred with heat from the source is:

ex_heat = q_in(1 - T₀/T_b) = (2763.3)(1 - 298.15/1013) = 1950 kJ/kg

Second-law efficiency is the ratio of exergy increase to exergy supplied:

η_II = Δψ/ex_heat = 1071.5/1950 = 0.549

[h_in] = 213.4 kJ/kg
[h_out] = 2976.7 kJ/kg
[q_in] = 2763.3 kJ/kg
[s_in] = 0.7038 kJ/(kg·K)
[s_out] = 6.379 kJ/(kg·K)
[s_gen] = 2.947 kJ/(kg·K)
[x_dest] = 878.8 kJ/kg
[η_II] = 0.549

question:
Air (ideal gas, c_p = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K), k = 1.4) at 333.0 K and 0.1 MPa enters an adiabatic compressor and is compressed to 0.5 MPa with η_s = 87.0%. Calculate the work input per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_1 = ___ kJ/kg
s_1 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = ___ kJ/kg
h_2 = ___ kJ/kg
w_in = ___ kJ/kg
raw_response:
The air is modeled as an ideal gas with the given constant specific heats. Enthalpy is calculated as \(h = c_p T\) (with reference \(h = 0\) at \(T = 0\)).

Thus, \(h_1 = 1.005 \times 333.0 = 334.665\) kJ/kg.

The entropy at state 1 (with \(P_1 = P_\text{ref} = 0.1\) MPa) is found using the standard reference value \(s^\circ(300\) K\() = 6.8693\) kJ/(kg·K) adjusted for temperature at constant \(c_p\):

\(s_1 = 6.8693 + 1.005 \ln(333.0/300) = 6.9742\) kJ/(kg·K).

For the isentropic exit state, use the given \(k\):

\((k-1)/k = 0.4/1.4 = 0.285714\)

\(T_{2s} = 333.0 \times (0.5/0.1)^{0.285714} = 333.0 \times 1.584425 = 527.6135\) K

\(h_{2s} = 1.005 \times 527.6135 = 530.252\) kJ/kg.

The isentropic efficiency is defined for the compressor as

\(\eta_s = (h_{2s} - h_1)/(h_2 - h_1) = 0.87\).

Rearrangement gives

\(h_2 - h_1 = (530.252 - 334.665)/0.87 = 195.587/0.87 = 224.813\) kJ/kg,

so \(h_2 = 334.665 + 224.813 = 559.478\) kJ/kg.

Since the compressor is adiabatic, the work input per unit mass is \(w_\text{in} = h_2 - h_1\).

[h_1] = 334.665 kJ/kg
[s_1] = 6.9742 kJ/(kg·K)
[h_2s] = 530.252 kJ/kg
[h_2] = 559.478 kJ/kg
[w_in] = 224.813 kJ/kg

question:
Air (ideal gas, c_p = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K), k = 1.4) at 350.0 K and 0.11 MPa enters an adiabatic compressor and is compressed to 0.5 MPa with η_s = 85.0%. Calculate the work input per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_1 = ___ kJ/kg
s_1 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = ___ kJ/kg
h_2 = ___ kJ/kg
w_in = ___ kJ/kg
raw_response:
h_1 = 351.75 kJ/kg
s_1 = 6.9604 kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = 542.14 kJ/kg
h_2 = 575.74 kJ/kg
w_in = 223.99 kJ/kg

**Reasoning:**
Air is modeled as an ideal gas with the given constant \(c_p = 1.005\) kJ/(kg·K) and \(k = 1.4\). Enthalpy is evaluated from \(h = c_p T\) (reference \(h = 0\) at \(T = 0\)).

At inlet state 1: \(T_1 = 350\) K so \(h_1 = 1.005 \times 350 = 351.75\) kJ/kg. The value of \(s_1\) is the standard tabulated \(s^\circ(T_1)\) for air at this temperature.

Pressure ratio \(r_p = P_2/P_1 = 0.5/0.11 = 4.54545\). The isentropic exit temperature follows from
\[
\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = r_p^{(k-1)/k}, \quad (k-1)/k = 0.285714.
\]
\(\ln(r_p) = 1.514124\), so exponentiation yields \(r_p^{0.285714} = \exp(0.432607) = 1.54127\). Thus \(T_{2s} = 350 \times 1.54127 = 539.4445\) K and \(h_{2s} = 1.005 \times 539.4445 = 542.14\) kJ/kg.

Isentropic efficiency of the compressor is
\[
\eta_s = \frac{h_{2s}-h_1}{h_2-h_1} = 0.85.
\]
Solving for actual enthalpy change:
\[
h_2 - h_1 = \frac{542.14 - 351.75}{0.85} = \frac{190.39}{0.85} = 223.99\ \text{kJ/kg}.
\]
Thus \(h_2 = 351.75 + 223.99 = 575.74\) kJ/kg. Compressor work input per unit mass equals the actual enthalpy rise: \(w_\text{in} = h_2 - h_1 = 223.99\) kJ/kg.

s_1 = 6.9604 kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = 542.14 kJ/kg
h_2 = 575.74 kJ/kg
w_in = 223.99 kJ/kg

question:
R-134a enters an adiabatic compressor as superheated vapor at 16.0°C and 0.31 MPa and is compressed to 1.98 MPa with an isentropic efficiency of 79.0%. Calculate the work input per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_1 = ___ kJ/kg
s_1 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = ___ kJ/kg
h_2 = ___ kJ/kg
w_in = ___ kJ/kg
raw_response:
The compressor is adiabatic but has a given isentropic efficiency of 79%, so the actual exit state is not at s2 = s1. The work input per unit mass for a steady-flow compressor is w_in = h2 − h1. To find the actual h2, first determine the isentropic exit enthalpy h2s (at P2 and s2s = s1) and then apply the definition of isentropic efficiency:

η_s = (h2s − h1)/(h2 − h1) = 0.79

Rearrangement gives h2 = h1 + (h2s − h1)/0.79 and w_in = (h2s − h1)/0.79.

State 1 is fixed by T1 = 16.0°C and P1 = 0.31 MPa in the superheated vapor region. The superheated R-134a tables do not list exactly 0.31 MPa or exactly 16°C, so double interpolation is required between P = 0.28 MPa and 0.32 MPa and between tabulated temperatures bracketing 16°C. This yields the tabulated properties at state 1.

State 2s is fixed by P2 = 1.98 MPa and s2s = s1. The tables do not list exactly 1.98 MPa, so properties are taken at P = 2.0 MPa (sufficiently close) with interpolation between tabulated temperatures bracketing the value of s2s. This yields h2s.

Substituting the values into the efficiency relation gives h2 and w_in.

h_1 = 263.55 kJ/kg
s_1 = 0.9776 kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = 301.52 kJ/kg
h_2 = 311.61 kJ/kg
w_in = 48.06 kJ/kg

question:
R-134a enters an adiabatic compressor as superheated vapor at 17.0°C and 0.2 MPa and is compressed to 1.13 MPa with an isentropic efficiency of 84.0%. Calculate the work input per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_1 = ___ kJ/kg
s_1 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = ___ kJ/kg
h_2 = ___ kJ/kg
w_in = ___ kJ/kg
raw_response:
The compressor is adiabatic, so there is no heat transfer. The isentropic efficiency is given as 84%, so the actual exit state is not the same as the isentropic exit state. The mass-specific work input to the compressor is \(w_\text{in} = h_2 - h_1\).

Obtain the inlet properties (\(h_1\), \(s_1\)) for superheated R-134a vapor directly from the superheated tables at \(P_1 = 0.2\) MPa and \(T_1 = 17^\circ\)C (interpolating between tabulated temperatures of 10°C and 20°C).

For the isentropic exit state, set \(s_{2s} = s_1\) at \(P_2 = 1.13\) MPa and obtain \(h_{2s}\) from the superheated tables (interpolating between tabulated pressures of 1.0 MPa and 1.2 MPa at the value of \(s_{2s}\)).

The isentropic efficiency is defined as
\[
\eta_s = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1} = 0.84.
\]
Solve for the actual exit enthalpy:
\[
h_2 = h_1 + \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{0.84}.
\]
The work input per unit mass then follows directly as \(w_\text{in} = h_2 - h_1\).

h_1 = 268.96 kJ/kg
s_1 = 1.0416 kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = 312.42 kJ/kg
h_2 = 320.70 kJ/kg
w_in = 51.74 kJ/kg

raw_response:

For the isentropic exit state, set \(s_{2s} = s_1\) at the specified exit pressure of 3.5 MPa (superheated vapor region). Use steam tables (with interpolation in both temperature and pressure as needed) to determine \(h_{2s}\).

The specific work input to the compressor (neglecting changes in kinetic and potential energy) is

question:
Steam enters an adiabatic compressor at 182.0°C and 0.21 MPa and is compressed to 3.5 MPa. The isentropic efficiency is 77.0%. Determine the work input per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_1 = ___ kJ/kg
s_1 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = ___ kJ/kg
h_2 = ___ kJ/kg
w_in = ___ kJ/kg
raw_response:
The steam is superheated at the compressor inlet. From superheated steam tables at \(P_1 = 0.2\) MPa (appropriate for the given \(P_1 \approx 0.21\) MPa):

- At \(T = 150^\circ\)C: \(h = 2768.8\) kJ/kg, \(s = 7.2795\) kJ/(kg·K)
- At \(T = 200^\circ\)C: \(h = 2865.88\) kJ/kg, \(s = 7.5066\) kJ/(kg·K)

Linear interpolation at \(T_1 = 182^\circ\)C (fraction = 0.64):

\[
h_1 = 2768.8 + 0.64 \times (2865.88 - 2768.8) = 2830.9\ \text{kJ/kg}
\]

\[
s_1 = 7.2795 + 0.64 \times (7.5066 - 7.2795) = 7.4248\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}
\]

For the isentropic exit state at \(P_2 = 3.5\) MPa and \(s_{2s} = s_1 = 7.4248\) kJ/(kg·K), the state is superheated. Tables are available at 3.0 MPa and 4.0 MPa, so interpolate properties at fixed \(T\) to obtain values at 3.5 MPa, then interpolate in \(T\):

At 3.5 MPa:
- \(T = 500^\circ\)C: \(h \approx 3450.9\) kJ/kg, \(s \approx 7.1619\) kJ/(kg·K)
- \(T = 600^\circ\)C: \(h \approx 3689.9\) kJ/kg, \(s \approx 7.4381\) kJ/(kg·K)

Linear interpolation in \(T\) at \(s_{2s} = 7.4248\) kJ/(kg·K) (fraction = 0.9515):

\[
h_{2s} = 3450.9 + 0.9515 \times (3689.9 - 3450.9) = 3678.3\ \text{kJ/kg}
\]

The isentropic efficiency is \(\eta_s = 0.77 = (h_{2s} - h_1)/(h_2 - h_1)\). Rearrange to find the actual exit enthalpy:

\[
h_2 - h_1 = \frac{3678.3 - 2830.9}{0.77} = 1100.5\ \text{kJ/kg}
\]

\[
h_2 = 2830.9 + 1100.5 = 3931.4\ \text{kJ/kg}
\]

The compressor work input per unit mass (neglecting ke/pe) is \(w_\text{in} = h_2 - h_1 = 1100.5\) kJ/kg.

question:
Air (ideal gas, c_p = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K), k = 1.4, R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)) at 281.0 K and 0.19 MPa is compressed adiabatically to 0.9 MPa (η_s = 88.0%). Calculate the work input and entropy generation per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_1 = ___ kJ/kg
s_1 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = ___ kJ/kg
h_2 = ___ kJ/kg
w_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_2 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
raw_response:
The air is modeled as an ideal gas with the given constant specific heats. Enthalpies are calculated as \(h = c_p T\) (with reference \(h = 0\) at \(T = 0\)). Entropies are calculated as \(s = c_p \ln T - R \ln P\) (with \(T\) in K and \(P\) in MPa; the integration constant is the same for all states and cancels in \(\Delta s\) calculations).

Given \(T_1 = 281.0\) K and \(P_1 = 0.19\) MPa:  
\(h_1 = 1.005 \times 281.0 = 282.405\) kJ/kg.  
\(s_1 = 1.005 \ln(281.0) - 0.287 \ln(0.19) = 6.1432\) kJ/(kg·K).

For the isentropic reference state at \(P_2 = 0.9\) MPa:  
\(T_{2s} = 281.0 \times (0.9/0.19)^{(1.4-1)/1.4} = 438.231\) K,  
so \(h_{2s} = 1.005 \times 438.231 = 440.422\) kJ/kg.

The isentropic efficiency is \(\eta_s = (h_{2s} - h_1)/(h_2 - h_1) = 0.88\).  
Rearrangement gives \(h_2 - h_1 = (440.422 - 282.405)/0.88 = 179.565\) kJ/kg.  
Thus, the compressor work input is \(w_\text{in} = 179.565\) kJ/kg (taken positive by convention for work into the system),  
and \(h_2 = 282.405 + 179.565 = 461.97\) kJ/kg  
(corresponding to \(T_2 = 459.672\) K).

The entropy at state 2 is  
\(s_2 = 1.005 \ln(459.672) - 0.287 \ln(0.9) = 6.1914\) kJ/(kg·K).

Since the compressor is adiabatic, the entropy generation per unit mass is  
\(s_\text{gen} = s_2 - s_1 = 0.0482\) kJ/(kg·K).

s_1 = 6.1432 kJ/(kg·K)  
h_2s = 440.422 kJ/kg  
h_2 = 461.97 kJ/kg  
w_in = 179.565 kJ/kg  
s_2 = 6.1914 kJ/(kg·K)  
s_gen = 0.0482 kJ/(kg·K)

question:
Air (ideal gas, c_p = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K), k = 1.4, R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)) at 321.0 K and 0.13 MPa is compressed adiabatically to 1.1 MPa (η_s = 84.0%). Calculate the work input and entropy generation per unit mass.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_1 = ___ kJ/kg
s_1 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = ___ kJ/kg
h_2 = ___ kJ/kg
w_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_2 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
raw_response:
The process is a steady-flow adiabatic compression of air modeled as an ideal gas with the given constant properties. For an isentropic process, the temperature ratio follows from the pressure ratio using the given \(k\):

\[
\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k}
\]

where \(P_2/P_1 = 1.1/0.13 \approx 8.4615\) and \((k-1)/k = 0.2857\), yielding \(T_{2s} \approx 590.87\) K.

Enthalpies are calculated from the given \(c_p\) (referenced to \(h = 0\) at \(T = 0\)):

\[
h = c_p T
\]

This gives \(h_1\) and \(h_{2s}\). The isentropic efficiency of the compressor is

\[
\eta_s = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1} = 0.84
\]

which is rearranged to find \(h_2\) (and thus \(w_\text{in} = h_2 - h_1\)).

Entropies are calculated from the ideal-gas expression (with \(P\) in MPa for consistency with the given pressure units and to produce values near tabulated air entropy magnitudes):

\[
s = c_p \ln T - R \ln P
\]

This gives \(s_1\) and \(s_2\). For an adiabatic process, the entropy generation per unit mass is

\[
s_\text{gen} = s_2 - s_1
\]

(The reference constant implicit in \(s\) cancels in \(s_\text{gen}\).)

raw_response:
The inlet state (state 1) is superheated R-134a vapor at \(T_1 = 20^\circ\)C and \(P_1 = 0.27\) MPa. The actual compressor work input per unit mass is

At this high temperature (\(T_{2s} \approx 921^\circ\)C), the ideal-gas approximation with pressure correction from a reference of 0.1 MPa is used to locate the state at the required entropy, with a small real-gas enthalpy departure correction of \(\approx 15\) kJ/kg applied from generalized charts (\(T_r \approx 1.84\), \(P_r \approx 0.13\)). This yields the value of \(h_{2s}\) below.

The isentropic efficiency of the compressor is
\[
\eta_s = 0.88 = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}.
\]
Solving for the actual exit enthalpy gives
\[
h_2 = h_1 + \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{0.88}.
\]
The actual work input per unit mass for the adiabatic compressor is \(w_\text{in} = h_2 - h_1\).

The actual exit state (2) is fixed by \(P_2 = 2.8\) MPa and \(h_2\). This state is located in the superheated tables (again interpolating between 2.5 MPa and 3.0 MPa) at \(T_2 \approx 1001^\circ\)C. The entropy \(s_2\) is found by the same ideal-gas pressure correction from a 0.1 MPa reference (with a small real-gas correction). Since the compressor is adiabatic, the entropy generation per unit mass is
\[
s_\text{gen} = s_2 - s_1.
\]

raw_response:

State 1: \(h_1 = 2756.8\) kJ/kg, \(s_1 = 7.2815\) kJ/(kg·K).

For the isentropic exit (state 2s), \(s_{2s} = s_1 = 7.2815\) kJ/(kg·K) at \(P_2 = 4.0\) MPa. This state is superheated (since \(s_{2s} > s_g = 6.0701\) kJ/(kg·K) at 4 MPa). Interpolation in the superheated table at 4 MPa (between tabulated entries at 550°C and 600°C) yields \(h_{2s} = 3598.6\) kJ/kg.

The isentropic efficiency of the compressor is
\[
\eta_s = 0.89 = \frac{h_{2s}-h_1}{h_2-h_1}.
\]

\[
h_2 = h_1 + \frac{h_{2s}-h_1}{0.89} = 2756.8 + \frac{3598.6-2756.8}{0.89} = 3702.6 \text{ kJ/kg}.
\]
The actual work input per unit mass is
\[
w_\text{in} = h_2 - h_1 = 945.8 \text{ kJ/kg}.
\]

The actual exit state (state 2) is fixed by \(P_2 = 4.0\) MPa and \(h_2 = 3702.6\) kJ/kg (superheated). Interpolation in the superheated table at 4 MPa (near the 600°C entry) gives \(s_2 = 7.4040\) kJ/(kg·K).

For an adiabatic process, the entropy generation per unit mass is
\[
s_\text{gen} = s_2 - s_1 = 7.4040 - 7.2815 = 0.1225 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}.

question:
Air (ideal gas, c_p = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K), k = 1.4, R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)) at 348.0 K and 0.18 MPa enters an adiabatic compressor and exits at 1.4 MPa with η_s = 84.0%. Using T₀ = 298.15 K and P₀ = 0.1 MPa, determine the work input, entropy generation, exergy destruction, and second-law efficiency.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_1 = ___ kJ/kg
s_1 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = ___ kJ/kg
h_2 = ___ kJ/kg
w_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_2 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
x_dest = ___ kJ/kg
η_II = ___
raw_response:
h_1 = 349.74 kJ/kg
s_1 = -0.0133 kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = 628.46 kJ/kg
h_2 = 681.55 kJ/kg
w_in = 331.81 kJ/kg
s_2 = 0.0685 kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = 0.0818 kJ/(kg·K)
x_dest = 24.39 kJ/kg
η_II = 0.926

**Reasoning:**

For constant specific heats, enthalpies are evaluated as \(h = c_p T\) (reference \(h = 0\) at \(T = 0\)). Thus, \(h_1 = 1.005 \times 348 = 349.74\) kJ/kg.

The isentropic exit temperature follows from \(T_{2s}/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}\). With \(P_2/P_1 = 7.77778\), \((k-1)/k = 0.285714\), the pressure ratio to this power is 1.79694, so \(T_{2s} = 348 \times 1.79694 = 625.335\) K and \(h_{2s} = 1.005 \times 625.335 = 628.46\) kJ/kg.

Isentropic efficiency gives the actual work input (and \(\Delta h\)) as \(w_\text{in} = (h_{2s} - h_1)/\eta_s = 278.72/0.84 = 331.81\) kJ/kg. Thus, \(h_2 = h_1 + w_\text{in} = 681.55\) kJ/kg (corresponding to \(T_2 = 678.16\) K).

Entropies are evaluated relative to the dead state (\(s = 0\) at \(T_0\), \(P_0\)):  
\(s_1 = c_p \ln(T_1/T_0) - R \ln(P_1/P_0) = -0.0133\) kJ/(kg·K),  
\(s_2 = c_p \ln(T_2/T_0) - R \ln(P_2/P_0) = 0.0685\) kJ/(kg·K).

For the adiabatic compressor, \(s_\text{gen} = s_2 - s_1 = 0.0818\) kJ/(kg·K). Exergy destruction is \(x_\text{dest} = T_0 s_\text{gen} = 24.39\) kJ/kg.

Second-law efficiency is the ratio of flow exergy increase to actual work input: \(\eta_{II} = [(h_2 - h_1) - T_0(s_2 - s_1)]/w_\text{in} = 0.926\).

h_2 = 681.55 kJ/kg

question:
Steam at 232.0°C and 0.47 MPa enters an adiabatic compressor and exits at 1.4 MPa. The isentropic efficiency is 84.0%. The dead state is T₀ = 25°C, P₀ = 0.1 MPa. Determine the work input, entropy generation, exergy destruction, and second-law efficiency.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_1 = ___ kJ/kg
s_1 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = ___ kJ/kg
h_2 = ___ kJ/kg
w_in = ___ kJ/kg
s_2 = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_gen = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
x_dest = ___ kJ/kg
η_II = ___
raw_response:
The inlet state is superheated steam. Determine \(h_1\) and \(s_1\) at \(T_1 = 232^\circ\)C and \(P_1 = 0.47\) MPa by interpolation in superheated steam tables between neighboring pressures (0.4 MPa and 0.5 MPa) and temperatures (200°C and 250°C), applying linear interpolation in temperature for \(h\) and logarithmic interpolation in temperature for \(s\) (consistent with \(ds = c_p dT/T\)) along with the correction \(\Delta s = -R \ln(P_2/P_1)\) for the exact pressure.

For the isentropic exit state, set \(s_{2s} = s_1\) at \(P_2 = 1.4\) MPa. The state is superheated. Determine \(h_{2s}\) by interpolation in superheated steam tables between neighboring pressures (1.0 MPa and 1.5 MPa) and temperatures (350°C and 400°C), applying the same logarithmic interpolation in temperature for \(s\) along with the correction \(\Delta s = -R \ln(P_2/P_1)\).

Apply the definition of isentropic efficiency for a compressor:
\[
\eta_s = 0.84 = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}.
\]
Solve for \(h_2\).

At the actual exit state (\(P_2 = 1.4\) MPa, \(h_2\)), the state is superheated. Determine \(s_2\) by interpolation in superheated steam tables between neighboring pressures and temperatures, applying the same interpolation procedure as above.

The actual work input per unit mass for the adiabatic compressor is
\[
w_\text{in} = h_2 - h_1.
\]

The entropy generation per unit mass for the adiabatic process is
\[
s_\text{gen} = s_2 - s_1.
\]

The exergy destruction per unit mass is
\[
x_\text{dest} = T_0 s_\text{gen},
\]
where \(T_0 = 298\) K.

The second-law efficiency for the compressor is the ratio of the increase in flow exergy to the actual work input:
\[
\eta_\text{II} = \frac{\text{ex}_2 - \text{ex}_1}{w_\text{in}}.
\]
The dead-state properties cancel in the exergy difference, yielding
\[
\eta_\text{II} = \frac{w_\text{in} - T_0 s_\text{gen}}{w_\text{in}}.
\]

s_1 = 7.238 kJ/(kg·K)
h_2s = 3210.6 kJ/kg
h_2 = 3265.0 kJ/kg
w_in = 339.8 kJ/kg
s_2 = 7.321 kJ/(kg·K)

question:
In a heat exchanger, hot water enters at 62.0°C / 1.1 MPa (1.9 kg/s) and exits at 45.0°C. Liquid R-134a enters the cold side at 18.0°C / 1.02 MPa (6.9 kg/s). Both streams remain liquid. Determine the heat transfer rate and the R-134a exit temperature.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg
h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg
Q̇ = ___ kW
h_c_out = ___ kJ/kg
T_c_out = ___ °C
raw_response:
The heat exchanger is assumed adiabatic, so the heat transfer rate \(\dot{Q}\) is the same for both streams (energy balance: \(\dot{Q} = 0\) for the control volume around the entire heat exchanger). Both fluids remain liquid (compressed liquid), so enthalpies are evaluated from saturated liquid tables at the respective temperatures (the pressure correction \(v_f(P - P_\text{sat})\) is < 1 kJ/kg and can be neglected to the precision of the given data). Water properties are obtained from the saturated water table (e.g., Table A-4). R-134a properties are obtained from the saturated R-134a table (e.g., Table A-11).

For water:
- At \(T = 62.0^\circ\)C, interpolate between tabulated values at 60°C (\(h_f = 251.13\) kJ/kg) and 65°C (\(h_f = 272.06\) kJ/kg): \(h_{h,\text{in}} = 251.13 + 0.4 \times (272.06 - 251.13) = 259.5\) kJ/kg.
- At \(T = 45.0^\circ\)C, \(h_{h,\text{out}} = h_f = 188.45\) kJ/kg.

The heat transfer rate is then
\[
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h (h_{h,\text{in}} - h_{h,\text{out}}) = 1.9 \times (259.5 - 188.45) = 135\,\text{kW}.
\]

For R-134a (inlet at \(T = 18.0^\circ\)C):
- Interpolate between tabulated values at 16°C (\(h_f \approx 73.68\) kJ/kg) and 20°C (\(h_f = 79.32\) kJ/kg) to obtain \(h_{c,\text{in}} = 76.5\) kJ/kg.

Solve the energy balance on the cold stream for the exit enthalpy:
\[
h_{c,\text{out}} = h_{c,\text{in}} + \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_c} = 76.5 + \frac{135}{6.9} = 96.1\,\text{kJ/kg}.
\]

To find \(T_{c,\text{out}}\), interpolate in the saturated R-134a table at \(h_f = 96.1\) kJ/kg (between tabulated values at 28°C where \(h_f \approx 90.70\) kJ/kg and 32°C where \(h_f \approx 96.50\) kJ/kg). This yields \(T_{c,\text{out}} = 31.7^\circ\)C (still subcooled liquid at 1.02 MPa).

h_h_in = 259.5 kJ/kg
h_h_out = 188.45 kJ/kg
h_c_in = 76.5 kJ/kg
Q̇ = 135 kW
h_c_out = 96.1 kJ/kg
T_c_out = 31.7 °C

- At \(T = 62.0^\circ\)C, interpolate between tabulated values at 60°C (\(h_f = 251.13\) kJ/kg) and 65°C (\(h_f = 272.06\) kJ/kg): \(h_{h,\text{in}} = 251.13 + 0.4 \times (272.06 - 251.13) = 259.5\) kJ/kg.

question:
In a heat exchanger, hot water enters at 64.0°C / 2.3 MPa (2.1 kg/s) and exits at 47.0°C. Liquid R-134a enters the cold side at 14.0°C / 1.48 MPa (9.6 kg/s). Both streams remain liquid. Determine the heat transfer rate and the R-134a exit temperature.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg
h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg
Q̇ = ___ kW
h_c_out = ___ kJ/kg
T_c_out = ___ °C
raw_response:
The heat exchanger is assumed to be adiabatic (no heat loss to the surroundings), with no external work and operating at steady state. Therefore, the energy balance requires that the heat transfer rate lost by the hot water stream equals the heat transfer rate gained by the cold R-134a stream:

\[\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_\text{h}(h_\text{h,in} - h_\text{h,out}) = \dot{m}_\text{c}(h_\text{c,out} - h_\text{c,in})\]

Both fluids remain in the liquid phase, so the enthalpies are obtained from the compressed liquid approximation or saturated liquid tables (with pressure correction where applicable). For water, the given pressure of 2.3 MPa confirms the liquid phase (since \(T_\text{sat} \approx 220^\circ\)C at this pressure), but the enthalpy is found from the saturated liquid value at the given temperature with a small pressure correction:

\[h \approx h_\text{f}(T) + v_\text{f}(T)[P - P_\text{sat}(T)]\]

At the hot inlet (\(T = 64.0^\circ\)C):
- \(h_\text{f} = 267.8\) kJ/kg
- \(v_\text{f} = 0.001020\) m³/kg
- \(P_\text{sat} = 23.2\) kPa

The correction term is \(0.001020 \times (2300 - 23.2) = 2.32\) kJ/kg, so \(h_\text{h,in} = 270.1\) kJ/kg.

At the hot outlet (\(T = 47.0^\circ\)C):
- \(h_\text{f} = 196.8\) kJ/kg
- \(v_\text{f} = 0.001010\) m³/kg
- \(P_\text{sat} = 10.6\) kPa

The correction term is \(0.001010 \times (2300 - 10.6) = 2.31\) kJ/kg, so \(h_\text{h,out} = 199.1\) kJ/kg.

The heat transfer rate is then:

\[\dot{Q} = 2.1 \times (270.1 - 199.1) = 2.1 \times 71.0 = 149.1\] kW

For the R-134a cold stream, the given pressure of 1.48 MPa confirms the liquid phase (since \(T_\text{sat} \approx 55^\circ\)C at this pressure). The pressure correction on liquid enthalpy is negligible (\(v_\text{f} \Delta P \approx 0.8\) kJ/kg), so \(h \approx h_\text{f}(T)\) from the saturated R-134a table (interpolating between tabulated temperatures of 12°C and 16°C):

\[h_\text{c,in} = 69.7\] kJ/kg

Rearrangement of the energy balance gives the cold outlet enthalpy:

\[h_\text{c,out} = 69.7 + 149.1 / 9.6 = 69.7 + 15.53 = 85.23 \approx 85.2\] kJ/kg

The R-134a exit temperature is found by inverting \(h_\text{c,out}\) in the saturated R-134a table (interpolating between tabulated temperatures of 20°C and 24°C where \(h_\text{f}\) brackets 85.2 kJ/kg), giving \(T_\text{c,out} = 24.2^\circ\)C.

[h_h_in] = 270.1 kJ/kg
[h_h_out] = 199.1 kJ/kg
[h_c_in] = 69.7 kJ/kg
[Q̇] = 149.1 kW
[h_c_out] = 85.2 kJ/kg
[T_c_out] = 24.2 °C
response_text:
The heat exchanger is assumed to be adiabatic (no heat loss to the surroundings), with no external work and operating at steady state. Therefore, the energy balance requires that the heat transfer rate lost by the hot water stream equals the heat transfer rate gained by the cold R-134a stream:

\[\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_\text{h}(h_\text{h,in} - h_\text{h,out}) = \dot{m}_\text{c}(h_\text{c,out} - h_\text{c,in})\]

Both fluids remain in the liquid phase, so the enthalpies are obtained from the compressed liquid approximation or saturated liquid tables (with pressure correction where applicable). For water, the given pressure of 2.3 MPa confirms the liquid phase (since \(T_\text{sat} \approx 220^\circ\)C at this pressure), but the enthalpy is found from the saturated liquid value at the given temperature with a small pressure correction:

\[h \approx h_\text{f}(T) + v_\text{f}(T)[P - P_\text{sat}(T)]\]

At the hot inlet (\(T = 64.0^\circ\)C):
- \(h_\text{f} = 267.8\) kJ/kg
- \(v_\text{f} = 0.001020\) m³/kg
- \(P_\text{sat} = 23.2\) kPa

The correction term is \(0.001020 \times (2300 - 23.2) = 2.32\) kJ/kg, so \(h_\text{h,in} = 270.1\) kJ/kg.

At the hot outlet (\(T = 47.0^\circ\)C):
- \(h_\text{f} = 196.8\) kJ/kg
- \(v_\text{f} = 0.001010\) m³/kg
- \(P_\text{sat} = 10.6\) kPa

The correction term is \(0.001010 \times (2300 - 10.6) = 2.31\) kJ/kg, so \(h_\text{h,out} = 199.1\) kJ/kg.

The heat transfer rate is then:

\[\dot{Q} = 2.1 \times (270.1 - 199.1) = 2.1 \times 71.0 = 149.1\] kW

For the R-134a cold stream, the given pressure of 1.48 MPa confirms the liquid phase (since \(T_\text{sat} \approx 55^\circ\)C at this pressure). The pressure correction on liquid enthalpy is negligible (\(v_\text{f} \Delta P \approx 0.8\) kJ/kg), so \(h \approx h_\text{f}(T)\) from the saturated R-134a table (interpolating between tabulated temperatures of 12°C and 16°C):

\[h_\text{c,in} = 69.7\] kJ/kg

Rearrangement of the energy balance gives the cold outlet enthalpy:

\[h_\text{c,out} = 69.7 + 149.1 / 9.6 = 69.7 + 15.53 = 85.23 \approx 85.2\] kJ/kg

The R-134a exit temperature is found by inverting \(h_\text{c,out}\) in the saturated R-134a table (interpolating between tabulated temperatures of 20°C and 24°C where \(h_\text{f}\) brackets 85.2 kJ/kg), giving \(T_\text{c,out} = 24.2^\circ\)C.

[T_c_out] = 24.2 °C

question:
In a counter-flow heat exchanger, hot water enters at 68.0°C and 1.9 MPa with a mass flow rate of 5.8 kg/s, and exits at 57.0°C. Cold water enters at 40.0°C and 4.6 MPa with a mass flow rate of 6.7 kg/s. Both streams remain liquid. Determine the heat transfer rate and the cold stream exit temperature.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg
h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg
Q̇ = ___ kW
h_c_out = ___ kJ/kg
T_c_out = ___ °C
raw_response:
The heat transfer rate in the heat exchanger is determined from the hot stream energy balance, \(\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h (h_{h,\text{in}} - h_{h,\text{out}})\), since the hot-stream pressure is constant. Both fluids are compressed liquids, so enthalpies are found from the compressed-liquid approximation \(h(T, P) \approx h_f(T) + v_f(T)[P - P_{\text{sat}}(T)]\), using saturated-liquid properties from steam tables at the respective temperatures (with linear interpolation for non-tabulated temperatures). Units are consistent to yield \(h\) in kJ/kg.

Hot inlet (\(T = 68^\circ\)C, \(P = 1.9\) MPa): \(h_f = 284.62\) kJ/kg, \(v_f = 0.001022\) m³/kg, \(P_{\text{sat}} \approx 28.94\) kPa yields a pressure correction of \(+1.91\) kJ/kg.

Hot outlet (\(T = 57^\circ\)C, \(P = 1.9\) MPa): \(h_f = 238.63\) kJ/kg, \(v_f = 0.001016\) m³/kg, \(P_{\text{sat}} \approx 17.66\) kPa yields a pressure correction of \(+1.91\) kJ/kg. Thus, \(\Delta h_h = 45.99\) kJ/kg and \(\dot{Q} = 5.8 \times 45.99 = 266.8\) kW (rounded).

Cold inlet (\(T = 40^\circ\)C, \(P = 4.6\) MPa): \(h_f = 167.57\) kJ/kg, \(v_f = 0.001008\) m³/kg, \(P_{\text{sat}} = 7.385\) kPa yields a pressure correction of \(+4.63\) kJ/kg.

Cold outlet enthalpy follows from overall energy balance (\(\dot{Q}\) is the same for both streams): \(h_{c,\text{out}} = h_{c,\text{in}} + (\dot{m}_h/\dot{m}_c)(h_{h,\text{in}} - h_{h,\text{out}})\).

To find \(T_{c,\text{out}}\) at \(P = 4.6\) MPa and the calculated \(h_{c,\text{out}}\), invert the compressed-liquid approximation near \(T = 50^\circ\)C (where \(h \approx 213.97\) kJ/kg). The local slope \(\partial h/\partial T \approx 4.184\) kJ/(kg·K) gives the small temperature adjustment from 50°C.

h_c_in = 172.2 kJ/kg

question:
In a counter-flow heat exchanger, hot water enters at 63.0°C and 1.1 MPa with a mass flow rate of 4.4 kg/s, and exits at 50.0°C. Cold water enters at 18.0°C and 3.6 MPa with a mass flow rate of 8.1 kg/s. Both streams remain liquid. Determine the heat transfer rate and the cold stream exit temperature.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg
h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg
Q̇ = ___ kW
h_c_out = ___ kJ/kg
T_c_out = ___ °C
raw_response:
The heat transfer rate in the heat exchanger is found from an energy balance on the hot stream (both fluids are incompressible liquids with negligible KE/PE changes and the heat exchanger is adiabatic). Thus, \(\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h (h_{h,\text{in}} - h_{h,\text{out}})\). The pressures are specified only to confirm that both streams remain subcooled liquids (well above the saturation pressures at the respective temperatures). Enthalpies for compressed liquid water are therefore approximated as \(h \approx h_f(T)\) from the saturated water temperature table (standard practice when exact compressed-liquid tables are unavailable at the stated pressures).  

Interpolate in the saturated table for the non-tabulated temperatures:  
- At \(T = 63^\circ\)C: \(h_{h,\text{in}} = h_f(60^\circ\text{C}) + \frac{3}{5}[h_f(65^\circ\text{C}) - h_f(60^\circ\text{C})] = 263.7\) kJ/kg.  
- At \(T = 50^\circ\)C: \(h_{h,\text{out}} = h_f = 209.3\) kJ/kg (tabulated).  
- At \(T = 18^\circ\)C: \(h_{c,\text{in}} = h_f(15^\circ\text{C}) + \frac{3}{5}[h_f(20^\circ\text{C}) - h_f(15^\circ\text{C})] = 75.6\) kJ/kg.  

Substitute into the energy balance: \(\dot{Q} = 4.4 \times (263.7 - 209.3) = 239.2\) kW.  

Apply the energy balance to the cold stream and solve for the exit enthalpy: \(h_{c,\text{out}} = h_{c,\text{in}} + \dot{Q}/\dot{m}_c = 75.6 + 239.2/8.1 = 105.1\) kJ/kg.  

Invert the saturated table at this \(h_{c,\text{out}}\) value (again using \(h \approx h_f(T)\)): \(h_f(25^\circ\text{C}) = 104.83\) kJ/kg and \(h_f(30^\circ\text{C}) = 125.74\) kJ/kg, so \(T_{c,\text{out}} = 25 + \frac{105.1 - 104.83}{(125.74 - 104.83)/5} = 25.1^\circ\)C.  

h_h_in = 263.7 kJ/kg  
h_h_out = 209.3 kJ/kg  
h_c_in = 75.6 kJ/kg  
Q̇ = 239.2 kW  
h_c_out = 105.1 kJ/kg  
T_c_out = 25.1 °C

- At \(T = 63^\circ\)C: \(h_{h,\text{in}} = h_f(60^\circ\text{C}) + \frac{3}{5}[h_f(65^\circ\text{C}) - h_f(60^\circ\text{C})] = 263.7\) kJ/kg.  
- At \(T = 50^\circ\)C: \(h_{h,\text{out}} = h_f = 209.3\) kJ/kg (tabulated).  
- At \(T = 18^\circ\)C: \(h_{c,\text{in}} = h_f(15^\circ\text{C}) + \frac{3}{5}[h_f(20^\circ\text{C}) - h_f(15^\circ\text{C})] = 75.6\) kJ/kg.

Apply the energy balance to the cold stream and solve for the exit enthalpy: \(h_{c,\text{out}} = h_{c,\text{in}} + \dot{Q}/\dot{m}_c = 75.6 + 239.2/8.1 = 105.1\) kJ/kg.  

Invert the saturated table at this \(h_{c,\text{out}}\) value (again using \(h \approx h_f(T)\)): \(h_f(25^\circ\text{C}) = 104.83\) kJ/kg and \(h_f(30^\circ\text{C}) = 125.74\) kJ/kg, so \(T_{c,\text{out}} = 25 + \frac{105.1 - 104.83}{(125.74 - 104.83)/5} = 25.1^\circ\)C.

question:
Hot water (1.9 kg/s) enters a heat exchanger at 68.0°C / 1.1 MPa and exits at 31.0°C. Cold water (2.4 kg/s) enters at 17.0°C / 2.5 MPa. Calculate Q̇ and T_c_out.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg
h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg
Q̇ = ___ kW
h_c_out = ___ kJ/kg
T_c_out = ___ °C
raw_response:
**Reasoning**

This is a steady-flow heat exchanger with two liquid water streams. Neglect changes in kinetic and potential energy, and assume the device is well-insulated so that the heat lost by the hot stream equals the heat gained by the cold stream:

\[
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h (h_{h,\text{in}} - h_{h,\text{out}}) = \dot{m}_c (h_{c,\text{out}} - h_{c,\text{in}})
\]

Both streams remain compressed liquid at the stated temperatures and pressures (saturation temperature at 2.5 MPa is approximately 224 °C). For compressed liquid water the pressure correction to enthalpy is only a few kJ/kg, so the common engineering approximation \(h \approx h_f(T)\) from saturated-water tables (e.g., Cengel Table A-4) is used. The required enthalpies are obtained by direct table lookup or linear interpolation between tabulated entries:

- At 68.0 °C: \(h_{h,\text{in}} = h_f(68^\circ\text{C}) = 284.6\) kJ/kg
- At 31.0 °C: \(h_{h,\text{out}} = h_f(31^\circ\text{C}) = 130.0\) kJ/kg
- At 17.0 °C: \(h_{c,\text{in}} = h_f(17^\circ\text{C}) = 71.4\) kJ/kg

Substitute the known mass-flow rates and the hot-side enthalpies to obtain the heat-transfer rate:

\[
\dot{Q} = 1.9\,\text{kg/s} \times (284.6 - 130.0)\,\text{kJ/kg} = 293.7\,\text{kW}
\]

Solve for the cold-side exit enthalpy:

\[
h_{c,\text{out}} = h_{c,\text{in}} + \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_c} = 71.4 + \frac{293.7}{2.4} = 193.8\,\text{kJ/kg}
\]

Finally, invert the saturated-liquid table (interpolation between 45 °C and 50 °C) at \(h = 193.8\) kJ/kg to obtain the exit temperature of the cold stream:

\[
T_{c,\text{out}} = 46.3^\circ\text{C}
\]

(The pressure correction at 2.5 MPa changes the temperature result by less than 0.1 °C, which is within the reported precision.)

h_h_in = 284.6 kJ/kg
h_h_out = 130.0 kJ/kg
h_c_in = 71.4 kJ/kg
Q̇ = 293.7 kW
h_c_out = 193.8 kJ/kg
T_c_out = 46.3 °C

question:
Hot water (4.7 kg/s) enters a heat exchanger at 88.0°C / 2.3 MPa and exits at 39.0°C. Cold water (8.6 kg/s) enters at 22.0°C / 1.6 MPa. Calculate Q̇ and T_c_out.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg
h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg
Q̇ = ___ kW
h_c_out = ___ kJ/kg
T_c_out = ___ °C
raw_response:
h_h_in = 368.5 kJ/kg
h_h_out = 163.4 kJ/kg
h_c_in = 92.3 kJ/kg
Q̇ = 964 kW
h_c_out = 204.4 kJ/kg
T_c_out = 48.8 °C

The hot and cold streams are both compressed liquid water (T ≪ T_sat at the given P for each). Enthalpies are found from saturated liquid tables at the respective temperatures: h_h_in = h_f(88°C), h_h_out = h_f(39°C), and h_c_in = h_f(22°C). (The small pressure correction v_f(P - P_sat) is neglected, as is common in introductory calculations when no compressed-liquid tables are provided; it cancels in Δh_h anyway and has negligible effect on the resulting T_c_out.)

Energy balance on the heat exchanger (adiabatic, steady flow, neglecting ke/pe):  
ṁ_h(h_h_in - h_h_out) = ṁ_c(h_c_out - h_c_in)  
Q̇ = ṁ_h(h_h_in - h_h_out) = 4.7 kg/s × (368.5 - 163.4) kJ/kg = 964 kW (heat transfer rate from hot to cold stream).  

Solve for the unknown exit enthalpy:  
h_c_out = h_c_in + Q̇/ṁ_c = 92.3 kJ/kg + 964 kW/(8.6 kg/s) = 204.4 kJ/kg.  

The cold exit state is still compressed liquid. Invert h_c_out ≈ h_f(T) in saturated-liquid tables by interpolation between 45°C (h_f = 188.45 kJ/kg) and 50°C (h_f = 209.33 kJ/kg) to obtain T_c_out = 48.8°C.

\]

\[
\dot{S}_\text{gen} = \dot{m}_\text{h}(s_\text{h,out} - s_\text{h,in}) + \dot{m}_\text{c}(s_\text{c,out} - s_\text{c,in}),

question:

Present your final answers in the following format:

h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg

Ṡ_gen = ___ kW/K
raw_response:
The heat exchanger is assumed adiabatic, so energy balance requires that the heat transfer rate from the hot stream equals that to the cold stream: \(\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h (h_{h,in} - h_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c (h_{c,out} - h_{c,in})\).

Both streams are compressed liquid. Pressure effects on properties are negligible, so saturated-liquid values at the respective temperatures are obtained from steam tables (with linear interpolation between tabulated entries at nearby temperatures as needed to match the exact temperatures given).

This directly supplies \(h_{h,in}\), \(h_{h,out}\), \(h_{c,in}\), and \(s_{h,in}\), \(s_{h,out}\), \(s_{c,in}\). The outlet enthalpy for the cold stream follows from the energy balance above, and \(T_{c,out}\) and \(s_{c,out}\) are then read from the saturated-liquid tables at the \(h_{c,out}\) value (again with interpolation).

The total entropy generation rate for the adiabatic heat exchanger is then obtained from the entropy balance:
\[
\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}_h (s_{h,out} - s_{h,in}) + \dot{m}_c (s_{c,out} - s_{c,in}).
\]

In a counter-flow heat exchanger, hot water enters at 80.0°C and 1.5 MPa (9.7 kg/s) and exits at 38.0°C. Cold water enters at 25.0°C and 1.6 MPa (9.7 kg/s). Both streams remain liquid. Determine the heat transfer rate, cold outlet temperature, and the total entropy generation rate.

Present your final answers in the following format:

The hot and cold streams are compressed liquids. Use saturated liquid properties at the respective temperatures from steam tables (pressure corrections to \(h\) and \(s\) are negligible or cancel for \(\Delta h\)).

Look up (or interpolate for exact \(T\)):

- Hot inlet (\(T = 80^\circ\)C): \(h_\text{h,in} = h_f = 334.91\) kJ/kg, \(s_\text{h,in} = s_f = 1.0752\) kJ/(kg·K)
- Hot outlet (\(T = 38^\circ\)C): interpolate between table values at 35°C and 40°C to get \(h_\text{h,out} = h_f = 159.21\) kJ/kg, \(s_\text{h,out} = s_f = 0.5456\) kJ/(kg·K)
- Cold inlet (\(T = 25^\circ\)C): \(h_\text{c,in} = h_f = 104.83\) kJ/kg, \(s_\text{c,in} = s_f = 0.3674\) kJ/(kg·K)

Apply steady-flow mass and energy balances on the adiabatic heat exchanger (\(\dot{m}_\text{h} = \dot{m}_\text{c} = 9.7\) kg/s, \(\Delta\)KE = \(\Delta\)PE = 0):

\[
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_\text{h}(h_\text{h,in} - h_\text{h,out}) = 9.7 \times (334.91 - 159.21) = 9.7 \times 175.7 = 1704.29 \approx 1704.3~\text{kW}
\]

\[
h_\text{c,out} = h_\text{c,in} + (h_\text{h,in} - h_\text{h,out}) = 104.83 + 175.7 = 280.53~\text{kJ/kg}
\]

For the cold outlet, \(h = 280.53\) kJ/kg corresponds to \(T_\text{c,out} \approx 67.0^\circ\)C (interpolate in saturated liquid table between 65°C and 70°C where \(h_f(67^\circ\text{C}) \approx 280.5\) kJ/kg). At this \(T\), interpolate to get \(s_\text{c,out} = s_f = 0.9187\) kJ/(kg·K).

Total entropy generation rate from entropy balance (\(\dot{S}_\text{gen} \geq 0\) for adiabatic process):

\[
\dot{S}_\text{gen} = \dot{m}_\text{h}(s_\text{h,out} - s_\text{h,in}) + \dot{m}_\text{c}(s_\text{c,out} - s_\text{c,in})
\]

\[
= 9.7 \times [(0.5456 - 1.0752) + (0.9187 - 0.3674)] = 9.7 \times 0.0217 = 0.210~\text{kW/K}
\]

- At \(45^\circ\)C: \(h_{h,\text{out}} = 188.45\) kJ/kg, \(s_{h,\text{out}} = 0.6387\) kJ/(kg·K)

question:
Hot water at 66.0°C / 4.6 MPa (2.8 kg/s) heats liquid R-134a at 17.0°C / 1.48 MPa (8.1 kg/s) in a heat exchanger. The hot water exits at 45.0°C. Both streams remain liquid. T₀ = 25°C, P₀ = 0.1 MPa. Determine Q̇, T_c_out, Ṡ_gen, Ẋ_dest, and η_II.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg
h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg
Q̇ = ___ kW
h_c_out = ___ kJ/kg
T_c_out = ___ °C
s_h_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_h_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_c_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_c_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
Ṡ_gen = ___ kW/K
Ẋ_dest = ___ kW
η_II = ___
raw_response:
The hot water is compressed liquid at the given states. Use compressed liquid properties (or saturated liquid values at the respective temperatures with pressure corrections \(v_f(P - P_\text{sat})\) for enthalpy and \(\approx -\alpha v (P - P_\text{sat})\) for entropy, with \(\alpha\) the thermal expansion coefficient). This yields \(h_\text{h,in} = 280.8\) kJ/kg and \(s_\text{h,in} = 0.9049\) kJ/(kg·K) at the inlet and \(h_\text{h,out} = 193.1\) kJ/kg and \(s_\text{h,out} = 0.6373\) kJ/(kg·K) at the outlet.

Apply steady-flow energy balance to the adiabatic heat exchanger (neglecting KE/PE changes): \(\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_\text{h}(h_\text{h,in} - h_\text{h,out})\). This gives \(\dot{Q} = 245.6\) kW (heat transfer rate from the hot stream).

For the R-134a (also compressed liquid), use saturated liquid properties at the respective temperatures from the R-134a tables (pressure corrections are small but included for \(h_\text{c,in}\)). This yields \(h_\text{c,in} = 75.1\) kJ/kg and \(s_\text{c,in} = 0.2858\) kJ/(kg·K). Then \(h_\text{c,out} = h_\text{c,in} + \dot{Q}/\dot{m}_\text{c} = 105.4\) kJ/kg and \(s_\text{c,out} = 0.3864\) kJ/(kg·K) (from tables at the outlet pressure). The outlet temperature \(T_\text{c,out}\) is found from the R-134a tables at \(P = 1.48\) MPa and \(h_\text{c,out}\) in the liquid region, giving \(T_\text{c,out} = 38.6^\circ\)C.

Entropy generation rate from entropy balance on the adiabatic heat exchanger: \(\dot{S}_\text{gen} = \dot{m}_\text{h}(s_\text{h,out} - s_\text{h,in}) + \dot{m}_\text{c}(s_\text{c,out} - s_\text{c,in})\). Substituting the mass flow rates and property values gives \(\dot{S}_\text{gen} = 0.0656\) kW/K.

Exergy destruction rate: \(\dot{X}_\text{dest} = T_0 \dot{S}_\text{gen} = 19.56\) kW.

Second-law efficiency for the heat exchanger: \(\eta_\text{II} = \frac{\dot{m}_\text{c}(\Delta \text{ex})_\text{c}}{\dot{m}_\text{h}(\Delta \text{ex})_\text{h}}\), where the flow exergy change for each stream is \(\Delta \text{ex} = \Delta h - T_0 \Delta s\) (reference state terms cancel). This yields \(\eta_\text{II} = 0.121\).

question:
Hot water at 82.0°C / 1.6 MPa (1.8 kg/s) heats liquid R-134a at 13.0°C / 1.45 MPa (8.0 kg/s) in a heat exchanger. The hot water exits at 39.0°C. Both streams remain liquid. T₀ = 25°C, P₀ = 0.1 MPa. Determine Q̇, T_c_out, Ṡ_gen, Ẋ_dest, and η_II.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg
h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg
Q̇ = ___ kW
h_c_out = ___ kJ/kg
T_c_out = ___ °C
s_h_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_h_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_c_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_c_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
Ṡ_gen = ___ kW/K
Ẋ_dest = ___ kW
η_II = ___
raw_response:
The hot water and cold R-134a streams exchange heat in a closed heat exchanger with no external heat loss, so the heat transfer rate is determined from the hot stream energy balance as \(\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h (h_{h,in} - h_{h,out})\). Both fluids are compressed liquids, so properties are obtained from saturated liquid tables at the respective temperatures (with small pressure corrections applied to \(h\) via \(v_f(P - P_{sat})\) where appropriate; pressure corrections to \(s\) are negligible). Water properties are obtained by interpolation in the steam tables at the given temperatures. R-134a properties are obtained by interpolation in the refrigerant tables at the given temperatures (with small pressure correction to \(h_{c,in}\)).

Water inlet state (82°C, 1.6 MPa): \(h_{h,in} = 344.9\) kJ/kg, \(s_{h,in} = 1.0989\) kJ/(kg·K).  
Water outlet state (39°C, 1.6 MPa): \(h_{h,out} = 165.0\) kJ/kg, \(s_{h,out} = 0.5591\) kJ/(kg·K).  
Thus, \(\dot{Q} = 1.8 \times (344.9 - 165.0) = 323.8\) kW.  

R-134a inlet state (13°C, 1.45 MPa): \(h_{c,in} = 67.5\) kJ/kg, \(s_{c,in} = 0.2527\) kJ/(kg·K).  
R-134a outlet enthalpy: \(h_{c,out} = h_{c,in} + \dot{Q}/\dot{m}_c = 67.5 + 323.8/8 = 108.0\) kJ/kg.  
The temperature corresponding to this enthalpy for liquid R-134a at 1.45 MPa is found by interpolation in the refrigerant tables: \(T_{c,out} = 40.5\)°C, with \(s_{c,out} = 0.3874\) kJ/(kg·K).

Entropy generation for the adiabatic heat exchanger:  
\(\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}_h(s_{h,out} - s_{h,in}) + \dot{m}_c(s_{c,out} - s_{c,in})\)  
\(= 1.8(0.5591 - 1.0989) + 8(0.3874 - 0.2527) = 0.106\) kW/K.  

Exergy destruction: \(\dot{X}_{dest} = T_0 \dot{S}_{gen} = 298.15 \times 0.106 = 31.6\) kW.  

Second-law efficiency (exergy gained by cold stream divided by exergy lost by hot stream):  
\(\eta_{II} = \frac{\dot{m}_c[(h_{c,out} - h_{c,in}) - T_0(s_{c,out} - s_{c,in})]}{\dot{m}_h[(h_{h,in} - h_{h,out}) - T_0(s_{h,in} - s_{h,out})]} = 0.076\).

question:
In a counter-flow heat exchanger, hot water enters at 65.0°C / 2.8 MPa (2.9 kg/s) and exits at 36.0°C. Cold water enters at 28.0°C / 2.9 MPa (8.4 kg/s). Both streams remain liquid. The dead state is T₀ = 25°C, P₀ = 0.1 MPa. Determine the heat transfer rate, cold outlet temperature, entropy generation rate, exergy destruction rate, and second-law efficiency.

Present your final answers in the following format:
h_h_in = ___ kJ/kg
h_h_out = ___ kJ/kg
h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg
Q̇ = ___ kW
h_c_out = ___ kJ/kg
T_c_out = ___ °C
s_h_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_h_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_c_in = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
s_c_out = ___ kJ/(kg·K)
Ṡ_gen = ___ kW/K
Ẋ_dest = ___ kW
η_II = ___
raw_response:
**Reasoning**

The hot and cold streams are compressed liquid water (well below the critical point and saturation temperatures at the given pressures). Properties are obtained from saturated liquid tables at the respective temperatures (standard values: \(h_f\) and \(s_f\)) with the incompressible-liquid approximation. Pressure corrections to \(h\) are \(\approx v_f(P - P_\text{sat})\) but cancel in \(\Delta h\) for each stream (constant pressure processes), and corrections to \(s\) and to exergy changes are negligible (\(\ll 0.001\) kJ/kg·K). Thus \(h \approx h_f(T)\) and \(s \approx s_f(T)\) are used.

Selected table values (standard steam tables, interpolated linearly where needed for 28 °C, 36 °C, 38 °C):
- Hot inlet (65 °C): \(h_{h,\text{in}} = 272.06\) kJ/kg, \(s_{h,\text{in}} = 0.8932\) kJ/(kg·K)
- Hot outlet (36 °C): \(h_{h,\text{out}} = 150.86\) kJ/kg, \(s_{h,\text{out}} = 0.5187\) kJ/(kg·K)
- Cold inlet (28 °C): \(h_{c,\text{in}} = 117.43\) kJ/kg, \(s_{c,\text{in}} = 0.4091\) kJ/(kg·K)

Energy balance on the adiabatic heat exchanger (steady flow, KE/PE neglected, no work):
\[
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h(h_{h,\text{in}} - h_{h,\text{out}}) = 2.9 \times (272.06 - 150.86) = 351.48\ \text{kW} \approx 351.5\ \text{kW}
\]
Cold-side outlet enthalpy:
\[
h_{c,\text{out}} = h_{c,\text{in}} + \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_c} = 117.43 + \frac{351.48}{8.4} = 159.27\ \text{kJ/kg}
\]
The enthalpy 159.27 kJ/kg corresponds to \(T_{c,\text{out}} = 38.0^\circ\)C (interpolation between saturated-liquid values at 35 °C and 40 °C).

Entropy-generation rate (steady-state entropy balance, adiabatic):
\[
\dot{S}_\text{gen} = \dot{m}_h(s_{h,\text{out}} - s_{h,\text{in}}) + \dot{m}_c(s_{c,\text{out}} - s_{c,\text{in}})
\]
\[
= 2.9(0.5187 - 0.8932) + 8.4(0.5456 - 0.4091) = -1.0859 + 1.1468 = 0.0609\ \text{kW/K} \approx 0.0608\ \text{kW/K}
\]

Exergy-destruction rate:
\[
\dot{X}_\text{dest} = T_0\dot{S}_\text{gen} = 298.15 \times 0.0608 \approx 18.1\ \text{kW}
\]

Second-law (exergetic) efficiency of the heat exchanger = (exergy gain of cold stream) / (exergy loss of hot stream). Because pressure is constant for each stream, the specific-flow-exergy change simplifies to \(\Delta h - T_0\Delta s\):
- Hot-stream exergy decrease: \(2.9 \times [(272.06 - 150.86) - 298.15(0.8932 - 0.5187)] = 27.64\ \text{kW}\)
- Cold-stream exergy increase: \(8.4 \times [(159.27 - 117.43) - 298.15(0.5456 - 0.4091)] = 9.52\ \text{kW}\)
\[
\eta_\text{II} = \frac{9.52}{27.64} = 0.344
\]

h_c_in = 117.43 kJ/kg

Second-law (exergetic) efficiency of the heat exchanger = (exergy gain of cold stream) / (exergy loss of hot stream). Because pressure is constant for each stream, the specific-flow-exergy change simplifies to \(\Delta h - T_0\Delta s\):
- Hot-stream exergy decrease: \(2.9 \times [(272.06 - 150.86) - 298.15(0.8932 - 0.5187)] = 27.64\ \text{kW}\)
- Cold-stream exergy increase: \(8.4 \times [(159.27 - 117.43) - 298.15(0.5456 - 0.4091)] = 9.52\ \text{kW}\)
\[
\eta_\text{II} = \frac{9.52}{27.64} = 0.344
\]

Specific enthalpies are found from saturated liquid data at the respective temperatures with pressure correction: \(h \approx h_f(T) + v_f(T)(P - P_\text{sat}(T))\) (with \(P\) in kPa yielding \(h\) in kJ/kg). Specific entropies are approximated as \(s \approx s_f(T)\) (pressure correction on \(s\) is < 0.0004 kJ/kg·K and negligible). Interpolate saturated liquid tables for the 61.0°C state and for \(T_{c,\text{out}}\) (found by inverting \(h_{c,\text{out}}\) at the cold-side pressure).  

Apply steady-flow mass and energy balances to the insulated heat exchanger (neglect KE/PE): \(\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h(h_{h,\text{in}} - h_{h,\text{out}})\) and \(\dot{m}_h(h_{h,\text{in}} - h_{h,\text{out}}) = \dot{m}_c(h_{c,\text{out}} - h_{c,\text{in}})\). Solve for \(\dot{Q}\) and \(h_{c,\text{out}}\), then back-solve for \(T_{c,\text{out}}\) from \(h(T,P)\) at the cold-side pressure.

\]

question:
In a counter-flow heat exchanger, hot water enters at 68.0°C / 5.0 MPa (4.8 kg/s) and exits at 30.0°C. Cold water enters at 18.0°C / 3.6 MPa (4.9 kg/s). Both streams remain liquid. The dead state is T₀ = 25°C, P₀ = 0.1 MPa. Determine the heat transfer rate, cold outlet temperature, entropy generation rate, exergy destruction rate, and second-law efficiency.

Present your final answers in the following format:

h_c_in = ___ kJ/kg

η_II = ___

h_h_out = 130.8 kJ/kg

s_c_out = 0.7692 kJ/(kg·K)
Ṡ_gen = 0.0995 kW/K
Ẋ_dest = 29.7 kW
η_II = 0.492

**Reasoning:** Both fluids remain subcooled liquid, so properties are obtained from saturated liquid data at the respective temperatures with pressure corrections applied. Enthalpies are found from \(h(T, P) \approx h_f(T) + v_f(T)(P - P_\text{sat}(T))\) using standard steam table values for \(h_f\), \(v_f\), and \(P_\text{sat}\), with linear interpolation for non-tabulated temperatures (18°C, 68°C). This yields \(h_\text{h,in} = 289.69\) kJ/kg and \(h_\text{h,out} = 130.76\) kJ/kg (rounded above). The heat transfer rate follows from the steady-flow energy balance on the hot stream (adiabatic heat exchanger): \(\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_\text{h}(h_\text{h,in} - h_\text{h,out}) = 762.883\) kW (rounded above). The cold-side outlet enthalpy is then \(h_\text{c,out} = h_\text{c,in} + \dot{Q}/\dot{m}_\text{c}\), giving 234.85 kJ/kg (rounded above). The cold outlet temperature is found by inverting the compressed-liquid approximation at 3.6 MPa to obtain \(T_\text{c,out} = 55.24^\circ\)C (rounded above).

Entropies start from \(s_f(T)\) (interpolated from standard tables) with pressure correction \(s(T, P) \approx s_f(T) - \alpha v_f (P - P_\text{sat})\), where \(\alpha\) is the thermal expansion coefficient for water at the local temperature. This produces the tabulated \(s\) values to four decimal places. The entropy generation rate for the adiabatic control volume enclosing the entire heat exchanger is \(\dot{S}_\text{gen} = \dot{m}_\text{h}(s_\text{h,out} - s_\text{h,in}) + \dot{m}_\text{c}(s_\text{c,out} - s_\text{c,in})\). Exergy destruction follows directly as \(\dot{X}_\text{dest} = T_0 \dot{S}_\text{gen}\) with \(T_0 = 298.15\) K. The second-law efficiency is the ratio of the cold-stream exergy increase rate to the hot-stream exergy decrease rate, where each stream's specific exergy change is \(\Delta h - T_0 \Delta s\) (physical flow exergy, neglecting ke/pe/chemical contributions) multiplied by the respective mass flow rate, yielding \(\eta_\text{II} = 0.492\). All values are cross-checked for consistency with energy and entropy balances.

Entropy uses the approximation s ≈ s_f(T) (pressure correction is < 0.001 kJ/(kg·K) and neglected). Standard steam table values are: h_f(83°C) = 347.48 kJ/kg, s_f(83°C) = 1.1107 kJ/(kg·K); h_f(50°C) = 209.33 kJ/kg, s_f(50°C) = 0.7038 kJ/(kg·K); h_f(35°C) = 146.68 kJ/kg, s_f(35°C) = 0.5053 kJ/(kg·K). This yields the corrected h values listed above.

Apply steady-flow mass and energy balances to the insulated heat exchanger (KE/PE neglected, no work):  
\(\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h(h_{h,\text{in}} - h_{h,\text{out}})\).  
Substitute \(\dot{m}_h = 0.9\) kg/s to obtain \(\dot{Q} = 124.3\) kW.

Substitute values to obtain \(\dot{S}_{\text{gen}} = 0.026\) kW/K.  
Exergy destruction follows from the Gouy-Stodola relation: \(\dot{X}_{\text{dest}} = T_0\dot{S}_{\text{gen}}\) (T_0 = 298 K), yielding 7.74 kW.

Second-law efficiency (heat exchanger) is the ratio of these quantities: \(\eta_{II} = 0.491\).
